Since this is the semi-annual report, we can assume that HIO will double the current earnings rate, which would leave us with an estimated net investment income of $0.32 per share by the end of the full year of 2025.

However, I am a bit unsatisfied with the level of net realized gains the fund has experienced. Over the last five years, HIO has experienced net realized losses more frequently than it has seen gains. Therefore, I worry about the fund's ability to participate in positive market momentum. I believe that the inconsistent performance here has contributed to HIO's NAV decline in recent years. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts going back to 2020:

  • 2020: $5.27 per share.
  • 2021: $5.45 per share.
  • 2022: $4.22 per share.
  • 2023: $4.15 per share.
  • 2024: $4.23 per share.
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $4.04 per share.

Since HIO's net realized gains are already negative for this first portion of 2025, I assume that performance won't substantially improve by the end of the fiscal year. Therefore, I anticipate that HIO's NAV will continue its downward slide in the short term. The declining NAV is one of the clearest indications that a fund's strategy isn't efficiently generating the earnings it needs, which makes it very difficult to recommend a position in HIO.

However, the overall appeal of the fund can be a bit tricky. HIO's price has declined by more than 68.4% since its inception. However, the total return including all distributions currently sits above 454%! Whether or not this type of capital deterioration is worth the tradeoff is up to investors to decide, but I do not think the value proposition is attractive in my opinion.

Risk Profile

The primary source of risk for a fund like HIO comes from the large exposure to debt securities that have poor credit ratings. For reference, credit ratings that are below a BBB are classified as below investment grade, otherwise known as 'junk bonds'. Borrowers with poor credit ratings typically have less consistent cash flows, rely more heavily on debt, and are less resilient to challenging market conditions. The usual relationship is that the lower the credit rating, the higher the chance of borrower default. HIO's breakdown is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 13.91%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - C): 83.39%
  • Not Rated: 2.10%

As you can see, HIO is primarily exposed to these below investment grade debt securities, which increases the overall risk for the fund. These risks are further increased by the higher interest rate environment. Looking at the graph below, we can see how the federal funds rate and HIO's price share an inverse relationship. When interest rates were cut to near zero levels following the pandemic, HIO's price rapidly increased. However, HIO's price remains heavily suppressed since interest rates were hiked to their decade highs throughout 2022 and 2023. A higher interest rate directly translates to a higher cost of debt.

A higher cost of debt negatively impacts operating margins for borrowers and makes it more difficult to grow. The higher interest expenses that borrowers need to pay, can also contribute to the rise in defaults as it becomes more difficult to keep up with the required debt maintenance. S&P Global estimates that the rate of defaults could increase to 1.6% in a base scenario by December 2025. However, I believe that the uncertainty around interest rate cuts and the tariff situation puts us near the pessimistic scenario, which estimates that defaults could rise as high as 3% by December 2025.

I believe that future interest rate cuts can serve as a strong positive catalyst for growth at some point in the future. Lower rates would increase optimism around debt securities and would result in the fear of defaults. Unfortunately, I don't believe interest rate cuts are on the horizon. I anticipate that the rise in costs due to tariffs will slow economic growth and cause an unfavorable shift in consumer spending. Inflationary data also remains slightly higher than the Fed's targeted 2% range, which is one of the reasons that the Fed decided to leave interest rates unchanged during their last meeting. Therefore, a higher for longer interest rate environment can limit the appeal of entry here and cause HIO's price to decline further if defaults increase.

Distribution Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0355 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11%. If we multiply this monthly payout by 12, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $0.426 per share. Referring back to the same semi-annual report, it would appear that HIO has a very inconsistent history of generating a sufficient amount of capital to support the distributions. Just for reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: $0.14 per share
  • 2021: $0.54 per share
  • 2022: ($0.87) per share
  • 2023: $0.30 per share
  • 2024: $0.51 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $0.02 per share

As we can see, HIO has only been able to out earn its distribution amount in two out of the last five years. The poor distribution coverage can certainly lead to a distribution cut in the near time if conditions do not improve. Looking over the payout history over the last decade, we can see that HIO has experienced a handful of reductions and raises in the past. Therefore, I wouldn't be surprised to see the fund reduce its distribution back to its prior 2023 levels to help preserve further NAV decay.

It's also worth mentioning that the distribution received from HIO is typically funded by net investment income. Therefore, the distributions would be mostly classified as ordinary dividend income, which has the heaviest tax burden for investors. So, based on your specific tax bracket, amount of capital invested, and desired income needs, a position in HIO would be best utilized within a tax advantaged account to offset any potential tax burdens.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate HIO as a sell for now. The underlying performance has been weak, but this could be due to the elevated interest rate environment. I do believe that lower interest rates can serve as a catalyst for growth at some point in the future, but the current environment does not warrant the need for cuts at this time. If we remain in a higher for longer interest rate environment, this could elevate the concerns around the underlying rate of defaults. Borrower defaults would negatively impact earnings and may cause HIO's price to fall further. The fund has demonstrated poor NAV growth over the last year and very inconsistent distribution coverage from earnings.
